title: Our Top 5 Fantasy Favorites: ‘Lord of the Rings,’ ‘House of the Dragon,’ and ‘Three Thousand Years of Longing’
format: George Miller's Three Thousand Years of Longing, House of the Dragon's premiere, and dueling top-5 fantasy-film lists, plus an interview with Funny Pages director Owen Klein.

segments.heading: Three Thousand Years of Longing
segments.note: George Miller's first film in seven years, since Mad Max: Fury Road

films.title: Three Thousand Years of Longing
films.note: Idris Elba's djinn tells Tilda Swinton's narratologist the story of his centuries of servitude in a hotel room in Istanbul. Adapted by Miller and his daughter Augusta Gore from an A.S. Byatt novella. Both hosts found the framing-device scenes between Elba and Swinton stronger than the fantastical flashbacks, and expect it to find its real audience on rewatch rather than in theaters.

segments.heading: Where Fantasy Comes From
segments.note: House of the Dragon just premiered on HBO; the hosts trace the genre back through its '80s boom

films.title: The Ten Commandments
films.note: Raised as a test case: is a biblical epic a fantasy film? Joanna says yes to this one, no to Passion of the Christ.

films.title: Conan the Barbarian
films.note: The film they credit with kicking off the '80s fantasy boom.

films.title: The Beastmaster
films.note: Part of the early-'80s cluster chasing Star Wars with swords instead of lasers.

films.title: Dragonslayer
films.note: Cited as a movie that hasn't aged well outside its own moment.

films.title: Willow
films.note: Comes up here as part of the genre history, then again as Joanna's own pick later.

films.title: Legend
films.note: Sean says it holds up mostly on Tim Curry's makeup.

films.title: The Princess Bride
films.note: The most popular '80s fantasy where they grew up, thanks to the Fred Savage/Peter Falk frame story.

films.title: The Black Cauldron
films.note: Disney's infamous flop that nearly sank its animation division.

segments.heading: Joanna's Top 5 Fantasy Films
segments.note: not a competitive draft, just favorites she wants to champion
segments.subject: Fantasy Films

films.title: The Fall
films.note: No. 1: Tarsem Singh's story-within-a-story, starring Lee Pace as an injured stuntman spinning a tale for a girl in his hospital. Joanna calls it the most beautiful film she's ever seen; it's also messy, shot piecemeal across whatever exotic locations Tarsem was scouting for commercials, and she says it's still not available to stream.

films.title: Wolfwalkers
films.note: No. 2 (a tie): Cartoon Saloon's Celtic-myth animated film, about a girl who can turn into a wolf. Joanna wanted it to win the animated Oscar.

films.title: Song of the Sea
films.note: No. 2 (a tie): the Cartoon Saloon film about selkies that Joanna paired with Wolfwalkers rather than pick just one.

films.title: Labyrinth
films.note: No. 3: Jim Henson's puppet fantasy with David Bowie as Jareth the Goblin King and a young Jennifer Connelly. Joanna says she didn't clock how psychosexual Bowie's performance is until she rewatched it as an adult.

films.title: Willow
films.note: No. 4: Ron Howard's film, from a story by George Lucas, starring Warwick Davis and Val Kilmer as Mad Martigan. Joanna watched it constantly as a kid, then couldn't find it anywhere for years until it landed on Disney+.

films.title: The Green Knight
films.note: No. 5: David Lowery's Arthurian retelling with Dev Patel as Gawain. Joanna's pick for most under-watched movie on the list.

segments.heading: Sean's Top 5 Fantasy Films
segments.note: his words: 'all of the fear and cowardice' compared to Joanna's list
segments.subject: Fantasy Films

films.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring
films.note: No. 1: Sean's pick over Return of the King, on the theory that Fellowship is the fandom consensus best of the trilogy. He singles out Sean Astin's Sam as the performance the movie doesn't work without.

films.title: Conan the Barbarian
films.note: No. 2: written by John Milius and Oliver Stone, directed by Milius. Sean rewatched it while prepping for The Book of Boba Fett and calls it uncomfortable by modern standards but still powerful.

films.title: The NeverEnding Story
films.note: No. 3: a shoutout to director Wolfgang Petersen, who died shortly before this episode.

films.title: Excalibur
films.note: No. 4: John Boorman's King Arthur film, with early roles for Liam Neeson, Gabriel Byrne, and Patrick Stewart, plus Nicol Williamson and Helen Mirren as Merlin and Morgana. Sean calls it a strange, LSD-trip of a movie.

films.title: Jason and the Argonauts
films.note: No. 5: not a great movie by Sean's own admission, but a foundational one for Ray Harryhausen's stop-motion effects work.

films.title: Funny Pages
films.note: Owen Klein's directorial debut, produced by the Safdie brothers, about a teenage cartoonist chasing mentorship in the world of underground comics. Klein talks about writing a part for his Meisner teacher, Louise Lasser, and shooting on 16mm to match the movies that made him want to make movies.

films.title: Artists and Models
films.note: Frank Tashlin's Martin and Lewis comedy. Klein's answer to the show's closing question, having just seen a 35mm IB Technicolor print at Lincoln Center.

films: Monty Python and the Holy Grail
